intro:
Alcuin of York (c. 735–804), English scholar and head of Charlemagne’s palace school, was the leading mind of the Carolingian renewal of learning. He reformed the liturgy, corrected the text of the Latin Bible, and wrote commentaries and educational works that schooled the medieval West.

As the architect of the Carolingian renaissance, Alcuin's chief service to the Bible was textual: he led the correction of the Latin Vulgate, whose drift through centuries of copying he worked to reverse, producing carefully edited pandects that stabilised the Western Bible for generations. His commentaries and teaching manuals trained the scholars who would carry patristic learning through the Middle Ages.
